words: VIOLA GUERNSEY: And he was so interested in the Palmer penmanship, the Palmer
system, graceful curves and swings. He was always practicing penmanship.
And he found that F C didn't make as graceful a signature as it would have
if it had been C.F. Thomas. So he legally had his name changed from Franklin
Charley to Charley Franklin so he could have a more impressive signature.
And he was a beautiful writer. His penmanship was beautiful, I wish she had
some of it with her, but she didn't have.

speaker: SS
words: He taught school...

speaker: VG
words: He taught school at Princeton.

speaker: SS
words: He probably gave the kids some good training in penmanship.

speaker: VG
words: Oh yes, he was teaching penmanship, that's primarily why he was interested

speaker: VG
words: in it, I suppose. Because we were speaking of the copy books we used to have.

speaker: VG
words: And how our teacher would write the top line in our copy book and then we'd

speaker: VG
words: do our best to copy it. So because of that system, I think I have changed

speaker: VG
words: my penmanship every time I have changed teachers. Nobody would be able to

speaker: VG
words: recognize my penmanship any more, that it, not through the years, I can

speaker: VG
words: see it in an old cookbook that I started years and years ago. The recipes

speaker: VG
words: from time to time are all together different. That is, the writing. But

speaker: VG
words: Chalice is a very nice penmanship writer too. She taught school for seven

speaker: VG
words: years before she moved to Aberdeen. That is, in this part of the county,

speaker: SS
words: You mentioned that you lived a number of places before you got to this area,

speaker: VG
words: Before we came to Princeton?

speaker: SS
words: How did it happen that you moved around and where did you go?

speaker: VG
words: I was born in Leigh Country, Iowa at Ft. Madison, And my dad was a locomotive

speaker: VG
words: engineer. And of course, he would be transferred from time to time, He

speaker: VG
words: worked for different railroads. And then he was with the Atchison,Topeka

speaker: VG
words: Santa Fe. And the Burlington, And so we moved from Iowa to Missouri, And then

speaker: VG
words: to New Mexico and then to Colorado, And then for twenty years that went on,

speaker: VG
words: our moving about from place to place. But my mother's home was in Illinois,

speaker: VG
words: And she had always wanted my dad to be a farmer, She had come from a farm

speaker: VG
words: in Illinois. Navoo was her town. And so he put his savings into a little

speaker: VG
words: canvas bag that mother made for him, whenever he had enough he'd put a twenty

speaker: VG
words: dollar gold piece in that little bag. And by and by he had two bags. They

speaker: VG
words: were about six inches high. And he had them in the back of an old

speaker: VG
words: that had been given to them for a wedding present, And I was not yet four

speaker: VG
words: years old, but I can remember climbing up on the table beside him, he pulled

speaker: VG
words: down all the curtains in the dining room and then he'd get down the clock

speaker: VG
words: and set it on the table and take out these two little bags and put in another

speaker: VG
words: twenty dollar gold piece. And I was facinated by that, Of course, I didn't

speaker: VG
words: know what it was all about, but I learned later. And when he had two thousand

speaker: VG
words: dollars in twenty dollar gold pieces, he bought a little farm in Nebraska.

speaker: VG
words: My mother had a brother who had taken^a homestead in Nebraska years before,

speaker: VG
words: He had six hundred af forty acres an his farm, uncle John Hummel, And father

speaker: VG
words: had just a small farm of two hundred acres, He sold the first little one

speaker: VG
words: before we moved there. It was six miles from town, from Lebanon, And then

speaker: VG
words: he bought a two hundred acre farm that was just across the road from Uncle

speaker: VG
words: John's farm. And it was all level prairie land, just a nice little place.

speaker: VG
words: And father had never farmed before. But he learned very fast, and he had

speaker: VG
words: ideas that he wanted to try,And he did very well, But then there came the

speaker: VG
words: time that the winds blew and blew and blew. Those who thought they knew

speaker: VG
words: said thatjjdust would continue blowing for seven years, I t blew the wheat

speaker: VG
words: right out of the ground and that dust bowl formed that time. There were many,

speaker: VG
words: many people in that area lost their farms. After several years of that dust

speaker: VG
words: bowl farming. But my dad wouldn't stay for that. After the first time his

speaker: VG
words: wheat blew out of the ground he said,"I finished here. We're going to Oregon,"

speaker: VG
words: And that was in 1909.

speaker: SS
words: What town was it near in Nebraska?

speaker: VG
words: Lebanon. We were four miles from Lebanon,

speaker: SS
words: He decided to leave?

speaker: VG
words: And we were there for four years. We had moved from Colorado Springs to

speaker: VG
words: Nebraska, Lebanon,Nebraska. And we stayed there four years and father didn't

speaker: VG
words: stay long enough to lose his farm or lose anything,We had money enough to

speaker: VG
words: come west and start over again. So he came in 1909, Ithink that was the

speaker: VG
words: summer of the Alaska, Yukon, Pacific Exposition in Seattle. He came out

speaker: VG
words: with his little Oxford bag, and Idon't knowJtow long he stayed. Perhaps

speaker: VG
words: two weeks. And he looked over all the country around Seattle and down in

speaker: VG
words: Oregon. He especially wanted to go to Oregon. And he found aplace that he

speaker: VG
words: thought he would like to be. It was near McMinville. And so that autumn when

speaker: VG
words: he came back, it was quite la* in the autumn. He had an auction and sold

speaker: VG
words: everything in the house. He had sold the stock too. And he said."We're going

speaker: VG
words: to sell everything except what goes in our suitcases and mother's

speaker: VG
words: trunk." And Iwas at school when the auction took place, And then in Feb

speaker: VG
words: ruary, as soon as the first semester, Iwas in my second year of high school

speaker: VG
words: and the first semester had ended, and it was sometime in February that we

speaker: VG
words: came to Oregon. To McMinnville.

speaker: VG
words: Did your father sell things that you missed?

speaker: VG
words: Yes, but Ihad to forget about them. There were some things Ithought about

speaker: VG
words: for long while afterward, but they were gone. The sale took place while

speaker: VG
words: Iwas in school. But anyway, then we stayed with Uncle John and Aunt Nettie

speaker: VG
words: until we were ready to leave. Yes, he sold the farm, the house, and every*

speaker: VG
words: thing. And we went to McMinnville and we were there about six weeks, I

speaker: VG
words: think, it was in February and it rained and rained and rained and it

speaker: VG
words: didn't do anything else but rain. Istarted to school there and my sister

speaker: VG
words: did too. That was all the family they had, ^ two girls. Ithought it

speaker: VG
words: was beautiful. Ijust loved all that rain, It had been so dry in Nebraska

speaker: VG
words: and the dust was blowing. And over in Oregon there were lemons on the trees

speaker: VG
words: both fruit and blossoms. And Ijust loved walking to school in the rain.

speaker: VG
words: My mother said she just couldn't -live there, that was all there was to

speaker: VG
words: it. It was too wet for her. And so father said,"Alright, if it Isn't

speaker: VG
words: pleasing to you, it isn't going to be to aplace for us to live and so

speaker: VG
words: we'll go farther in land,"And they came to Moscow, They knew a. family there

speaker: VG
words: in Moscow, by the name of Eggland, Susie and Peter Eggland, And they had a

speaker: VG
words: ten acre apple orchard, Rome beauties and other kinds of apples. Beautiful

speaker: VG
words: .and they kept it nice and clean. Because Susie would go out in the spring

speaker: VG
words: and she would hoe for, I think it was six or ten feet around each one of

speaker: VG
words: those trees and ten acres. And that was their farming, was that apple orchard

speaker: VG
words: And then we had rented rooms in Moscow, and I started to school again in

speaker: VG
words: Moscow high school. And father started going out with real estate agents

speaker: VG
words: to different places around outside of Moscow, and even came over here, as

speaker: VG
words: far as Princeton. And he liked a farm up towards Gold Hill, So he bought

speaker: VG
words: a hundred and twenty acres, I think up there near Gold Hill, And I didn't

speaker: VG
words: see it until June. The family came over here and moved into a litile up and

speaker: VG
words: down house that was on the place. They built a new house later. But for the

speaker: VG
words: time being, this was a "three room shack is what they called it, from Mr,

speaker: VG
words: and Mrs. Charlie Lemon, And the Lemons moved down to Princeton and my

speaker: VG
words: mother and father and sister moved into the little shack up the farm, And

speaker: VG
words: there were forty acres that had to be cleared. It was all in pine trees and

speaker: VG
words: fir trees. And there was forty acres cleared and that was what he was farming.

speaker: VG
words: And^he had my uncle to slash off another forty and that wood was just burned

speaker: VG
words: up,to get it out of the way to make a place to plant crops. Course, it took

speaker: VG
words: time. And then the other forty my father cleared most of that himself. That

speaker: VG
words: made the three forties. And they built the house up on the hill on the third

speaker: VG
words: forty. They used to go up there and my mother said,"This is such a beautiful

speaker: VG
words: spot. I'd like to build our house up here." It was on top of the hill. So

speaker: VG
words: father said,"Alright, we'll build it up here,'* and he had a contractor to

speaker: VG
words: move up here, that was in 1913, Three years after they had come to Princeton,

speaker: SS
words: You finished up the school year in Moscow and then moved with the family?

speaker: VG
words: Then I went up to the Ursuline convent. And I went to school there for the

speaker: VG
words: next three years.

speaker: SS
words: Where exactly was that?

speaker: VG
words: Ursuline Convent in Moscow, It's St. Mary's Academy now. And I was graduated

speaker: VG
words: from there in 1912. And then I had always wanted to teach school and so

speaker: VG
words: Mo ther Mary Rose gave some special credits and some tutoring for

speaker: VG
words: that required for teaching. And then right from the convent with those

speaker: VG
words: credits that Mother Rose had arranged for me, I had a scholarship and I could

speaker: VG
words: have entered the university then, but I wanted to teach a year first, And

speaker: VG
words: I went to Colfax and took the teachers examination. In the spring of 1913,

speaker: VG
words: and then all the schools by that time in Latah county seemed to be taken,

speaker: VG
words: contracted for. So I kept writing out of the state, I even wrote to Alaska

speaker: VG
words: for a position, and I wrote to several other places, but one of them was

speaker: VG
words: accepted at Creston, Washington, So I went out there to teach that term of

speaker: VG
words: school. It was thirteen miles from Creston and thirteen miles from Wilbur,

speaker: VG
words: out in the scabrock country, but that was a different experience and I enjoyed

speaker: VG
words: it. I taught that term sixty five dollars a month,

speaker: SS
words: What was your first year like?

speaker: VG
words: one room school house, and I had to walk a mile down one hill and

speaker: VG
words: up another. And there were fourteen pupils from grade one through eight. And

speaker: VG
words: what I watched most for was the mail carrier who drove in from Creston, He'd

speaker: VG
words: get there long about noon. Because I got a letter every day from*-Evan. And

speaker: VG
words: I don't think he ever missed a day sending a letter, In all that time I was

speaker: VG
words: there.

speaker: SS
words: When had you met him?

speaker: VG
words: Oh, in 1910, the summer of 1910, when I came home in June, My family lived

speaker: VG
words: uptfiere on the farm, And his family lived down at Princeton, His father

speaker: VG
words: was post master there for thirty five years, And he had a. general store,

speaker: VG
words: And I think it was at Sunday School that I met him. And I thought he was

speaker: VG
words: very clever, because when they passed around their plate, he would have a

speaker: VG
words: nickle balanced on each one of " fingers and he'd put 'em in like that.

speaker: VG
words: I'd never seen anyone do that before,(chuckles)But I think that was where

speaker: VG
words: I met him. Arid they had the Sunday School in the schoolhouse. There wasn't

speaker: VG
words: any church built there. In this town then,

speaker: SS
words: He wrote to you every day?

speaker: VG
words: I don't think he missed a day,

speaker: SS
words: He must have liked you.

speaker: VG
words: Well I don't know whether he said he did or not,but he wrote the letters

speaker: VG
words: anyway. They were just little letters. Just little friendly letters. They

speaker: VG
words: weren't romantic or amourous or anything. But I still have them tied up with

speaker: VG
words: a little red ribbon.

speaker: SS
words: It sounds lika an isolated time, teaching in the middle of nowhere, away

speaker: SS
words: from home. It sounds like it wouldn't be the easiest job

speaker: VG
words: I didn't expect it to be easy, I went there to teach and to work, And I

speaker: VG
words: boarded with a very nice family who lived a mile from the schoolhouse, And

speaker: VG
words: three of their children went to school, And we had programs at the school,

speaker: VG
words: All the people who had children would come, And we songfests and dialogs

speaker: VG
words: and such things that they had in school on Friday afternoons, They used to

speaker: VG
words: have those at Princeton too.

speaker: SS
words: Would there be parties at the school?

speaker: VG
words: No, the neighbors were so far apart, So every day was like a party at the

speaker: VG
words: school. There wasn't any well at the school house, there was a little wood

speaker: VG
words: shed of sorts where they had a little wood for kindling and coal, We just

speaker: VG
words: had to heat the school house with a little iron stove, And I used to carry

speaker: VG
words: a little ten pound lard bucket full of water from home. The children brogght

speaker: VG
words: their drinking water in bottles to school with their lunch. And I brought

speaker: VG
words: this little bucket full of water to pour over their hands before they would

speaker: VG
words: eat their lunch. They would all stand in a row outside in the schoolyard

speaker: VG
words: hold out their hands and I 'd pour a little bit of water on each one. And

speaker: VG
words: so they could rinse them off clean enough 4 eat their sandwiches.

speaker: VG
words: And that was all the water that we had there, unless somebody hauled some

speaker: VG
words: to clean the schoolhouse. They did that from time to time,Otherwise? I would

speaker: VG
words: just sweep it out and dust it, It was a new schoolhouse though, it was nice

speaker: VG
words: schoolhouse,painted white. But I think we had a Hallowe'en program

speaker: VG
words: and we had a Christmas program. And I think that was about the get togethers

speaker: VG
words: that we had,

speaker: SS
words: What did you think of the teaching there, was it difficult?

speaker: VG
words: No, it, there too many in any one grade. There were three big boys

speaker: VG
words: in the eighth grade. Their folks would keep them out to help with the farm

speaker: VG
words: work. Too much of the time. So they didn't pass and they weren't there enough

speaker: VG
words: really to make it count. I don't know what they did after I was there. I didn't

speaker: VG
words: come back the second year. But those dear little girls, there were three

speaker: VG
words: little girls in the fourth grade. They'd come with their sunbonnets on, I

speaker: VG
words: took pictittres of them out in the schoolyard, I still have all those. And there

speaker: VG
words: were two in the first grade, I think Hallowe'en, I don't remember just what

speaker: VG
words: we did. We had a basket social, that was it. We wanted the money to buy more

speaker: VG
words: library books. We had a little school library, That's what the Hallowe'en

speaker: VG
words: was for. A basket social, I don't remember how much we mades but we bought

speaker: VG
words: quite a few books, were not nearly so expensive then as they are now,

speaker: SS
words: Did you get a chance to come home very often?

speaker: VG
words: I came home at Christmas time, was all. Two weeks,

speaker: SS
words: But you didn't go back the next year?

speaker: VG
words: I went back the second term,

speaker: SS
words: The second year?

speaker: VG
words: No, I didn't go back for the second year, I taught the two semesters, And

speaker: VG
words: when I went home for Christmas, my folks had moved into their new house up

speaker: VG
words: on the hill, It was very nice up there

speaker: SS
words: Then what did you do after you stopped teaching?

speaker: VG
words: I came home in May and I had a month to make a trousseau and got married in

speaker: VG
words: June. And I've been married now, sixty two years. It will be sixty two years

speaker: VG
words: the eleventh of June, To the very same Evan,

speaker: SS
words: Where were you married?

speaker: VG
words: In my parents' home. Up on the hill. And Evan brought over Rev, Johnathon

speaker: VG
words: Watson from Moscow to perform the ceremony. He was an Episcopalian minister.

speaker: VG
words: And we had twenty five guests. They all, no they weren't all from Princeton,

speaker: VG
words: There were girls from over at Moscow, And Chalice played the wedding music.

speaker: VG
words: Chalice Thomas. She lived just across the road up on the other hill, And

speaker: VG
words: she had just come home from normal school at Cheney, Went to school at Cheney,

speaker: VG
words: She wanted to teach too. But I had always wanted to teach school. And I had

speaker: VG
words: planned to be an old maid. That was all I had ever wanted to do To be a

speaker: VG
words: school teacher. And Evan said,"Go ahead and t-each your school, I'll wait for

speaker: VG
words: you." And so he did. And I went and taught a year at least. And, that time

speaker: VG
words: I thought maybe it wasn't quite so romantic to teach school after all. But

speaker: VG
words: I wouldn't have gone on if it hadn't have been for him,I would have found

speaker: VG
words: a school somewhere else. But I wouldn't have gone back to Creston, It was

speaker: VG
words: rather bleak and not very exciting there, I never went anywhere all the

speaker: VG
words: time I was there. I just stayed out at th at place. Boarded for twenty dollars

speaker: VG
words: a month. But I like that family, they were very nice to me, And I roomed

speaker: VG
words: with their daughter. We had a little room upstairs. And we had to sleep

speaker: VG
words: between two featherbeds. So that was an experience too, Pauline was about

speaker: VG
words: the same age that I was. There name was Blenns, Mr, and Mrs, John Blenns,

speaker: VG
words: And their four children,

speaker: SS
words: Where did you get the idea to be a teacher?

speaker: VG
words: I think in Nebraska, My mother and father, they lived, when we first went

speaker: VG
words: to Nebraska, there was a little sod schoolhouse dn one corner of that farm*

speaker: VG
words: There was an acre taken out of that farm for a schoolyard, And there was

speaker: VG
words: a sod schoolhouse on it. And that summer some of the men in the school district

speaker: VG
words: took down that soddy schoolhouse and they built a new little white frame

speaker: VG
words: schoolhouse. And it was just a charming little place, It wasn't so hard to

speaker: VG
words: get water there and there were just a few children around the neighborhood

speaker: VG
words: A few who sent their children there, And there was my cousin and

speaker: VG
words: then the two girls in our family. And three other families, each had one

speaker: VG
words: child there and the Hague family had about six children who came, They had

speaker: VG
words: ten in their family, the Hague family. And we'd play ball at recess and we

speaker: VG
words: had such a good time, And my mother and father always boarded the school

speaker: VG
words: teacher. And I think I was in the sixth grade when we came there from

speaker: VG
words: Colorado Springs, And so there was the sixth and the seventh and the eighth

speaker: VG
words: grade and those schoolteachers all boarded with my father and mother. And

speaker: VG
words: I admired them so much.all my schoolteachers. I just thought, I'd like to

speaker: VG
words: be a schoolteacher too. So that's the way it came about, I think, For

speaker: VG
words: our eighth grade graduation, we went to the county,

speaker: SS
words: All rural schools in the county,

speaker: VG
words: All had their eighth grade graduation there. That was really quite an event,

speaker: VG
words: We went on the train fromo.ur town to McCook, to the county seat. And my

speaker: VG
words: mother had a white dress made by a dressmaker for me to wear, And all the

speaker: VG
words: girls wore white dresses and the boys all wore suits, I have the

speaker: VG
words: picture somewhere tucked away of our eighth grade class from all over the

speaker: VG
words: county. That was really quite an event,

speaker: SS
words: Did you find moving from place to place to be difficult for you?

speaker: VG
words: I often, I wouldn't say often, but I used to wish that I could lifce in one

speaker: VG
words: town all my life and know everybody and not have to make new friends every

speaker: VG
words: where I went. Yes, I did. I did notice that, But then, children adjust

speaker: VG
words: easily. They quickly find new interests and new friends,

speaker: SS
words: Seems since you've settled here, you've spent your adult life in one place,

speaker: VG
words: Yes, yes this had been just idyllic, There was one friend, there is one

speaker: VG
words: friend in Colorado Springs, we went to the same Sunday School, Her name

speaker: VG
words: was Merdices Hoover. And just about a year after we moved to Nebraska from

speaker: VG
words: Colorado Springs, her family moved to Seattle, And we still call each other

speaker: VG
words: on the telephone and write letters. She graduated from high school in Seattle

speaker: VG
words: and then from the university from Washington. And she became a professor in the school

speaker: VG
words: of arts,And she had her

speaker: VG
words: master's degree in five arts. And she taught there until she was seventy,

speaker: VG
words: I think. Until retirement age. I think in Washington at 65 but you're

speaker: VG
words: retired at 70. So she stayed the full time. Her husband died after they had

speaker: VG
words: their fiftieth anniversary. She had married Perry Hensley just after they

speaker: VG
words: were out of high school. And I used to go over to visit them, I went to

speaker: VG
words: visit them once while I was at Creston, That was a happy break that I had

speaker: VG
words: while I was over there, At Thanksgiving time I went to Cooley City where

speaker: VG
words: she and Perry had,built themselves a home on his father's ranch. He wanted

speaker: VG
words: to have a ranch for each of his boys.And they were to have all the increase

speaker: VG
words: of the stock. It was a cattle ranch,Up in Moses Cooley, I think maybe it's

speaker: VG
words: underwater now. No, it isn't either, Because she has been back there since

speaker: VG
words: and there's nothing left of the house or the barn or anything. But I went

speaker: VG
words: and stayed with them that weekend of Thanksgiving, lirent on the train, And

speaker: VG
words: that was the only break I had during that term of teaching.

speaker: SS
words: Probably of the people around Creston, quite a few were homesteaders?

speaker: VG
words: They may have been, I don't know about that. But they were right at the edge

speaker: VG
words: of the scabrock country. They had, they cut hay en all the creek bottoms,

speaker: VG
words: And I think they had a little crop land, but not very much, They didn't

speaker: VG
words: have very much grain. It was rocky country, You know, that part of Washington

speaker: VG
words: is. It's just beyound the wheat country. They raised turkeyAand they raised

speaker: VG
words: stock. And it seems as if down in that valley along the creek bottom, they

speaker: VG
words: had^hay for their stock.

speaker: SS
words: What was the Ursuline Academy like?

speaker: VG
words: It was a girls' boarding school. And they had girls from kindergarten age

speaker: VG
words: on through high school,The little kindergarten people didn't board there

speaker: VG
words: when I was there. just high school girls. But there were three nuns

speaker: VG
words: who operated that school,There was Mother Mary Rose and Sister Carmel and

speaker: VG
words: Mother Paula, she was the music teacher. And they had CL day school also

speaker: VG
words: for,it was a parochial school, mostly Catholics, but I wasn't a Catholic.

speaker: VG
words: But I had gone to Catholic academy for my first year in high school, I went

speaker: VG
words: from Nebraska back to Illinois, to St. Mary's Academy, Because that

speaker: VG
words: was where my mother had grown up in Nalfcoo and she liked that school and s/ye

speaker: VG
words: wanted me to go there. And this was very similar, but it was a smaller

speaker: VG
words: school. The one that Moscow had^ I don't rememberAhow many, but they had all

speaker: VG
words: the grades. And they had the three nuns taught school and they had other

speaker: VG
words: teachers besides.

speaker: SS
words: Was there much religious instruction?

speaker: VG
words: Not for me. You weren't required to take the religious instruction if you

speaker: VG
words: weren't a Catholic and didn't care for it

speaker: SS
words: Was the studying difficult?

speaker: VG
words: Thejfr were regular high school subjects, all of them, Because it was an

speaker: VG

speaker: VG

speaker: VG
